article.title: New "Bad Epoll" Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Unprivileged Users Gain Root, Hits Android
article.description: A newly disclosed Linux kernel flaw called Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242) lets an ordinary user with no special access take full control of a machine as root. It affects Linux desktops, servers, and Android, and a fix is out. Bad Epoll sits in the same small stretch of kernel code where Anthropic's most powerful AI model, Mythos, recently found a different bug. The AI caught one flaw and missed

generated.caption:
Here's an Instagram caption for CyberShield Alerts:

Your Android phone or Linux PC could be at risk from a new serious flaw! 🚨

A critical new security vulnerability, dubbed "Bad Epoll" (CVE-2026-46242), has been discovered in the core of the Linux operating system. This flaw allows an ordinary user without any special permissions to take complete control of a vulnerable machine, gaining "root" access.

This affects a wide range of systems, including Linux desktops, servers, and popular Android devices.

The good news? A fix has already been released! So, make sure your systems are updated promptly to protect yourself.

Interestingly, this bug was found in the same section of code where Anthropic's powerful AI model, Mythos, previously identified a *different* vulnerability. While Mythos caught one, it didn't spot "Bad Epoll" itself – highlighting the ongoing complexity of security even with advanced AI tools.

Stay safe and keep your software updated! 💪
Double-tap if you found this useful! 👇 Save this post to remember to check your updates, and share it with someone who uses Linux or Android. Follow @CyberShieldAlerts for daily cybersecurity and AI security insights!

slides.text: New Linux Flaw: Unprivileged Users Can Gain Root!

slides.heading: What is 'Bad Epoll'?
slides.text: A critical new Linux kernel flaw, officially known as CVE-2026-46242, has just been revealed.

slides.heading: Full Control for Attackers!
slides.text: This 'Bad Epoll' vulnerability allows an ordinary user, even without special permissions, to gain complete root control of your machine.

slides.heading: Who's Affected?
slides.text: Linux desktops, servers, and even Android devices running vulnerable kernel versions are all at risk from this flaw.

slides.heading: Good News: Fix Is Out!
slides.text: The good news? A patch to fix the Bad Epoll vulnerability has already been released. Update your systems now!
Stay secure! Follow @CyberShieldAlerts for critical updates and tips.
